OK. OK. It's got a substantial submission. Yeah. Ask more questions. Ask more questions. Yeah. I don't know if that were real. Yeah. Yeah. Yeah. Yeah. Yeah. Yeah. Yeah. Julie, do we think there's... Can you pop in to accept us? Julie, is it?

Yeah. Julie, online, if you can hear us, and if you're from Putiraco Trust, please. Would you accept the... the request that's been sent through to you to join us? Kia ora, Julie. Kia ora. Thank you very much for popping along. We haven't seen Jason as yet, but if you are happy

to proceed and provide the verbal submission on behalf of your entity, be happy for that to begin if you wish. Thank you. I have been delegated authority as Jason a responsible committee member is in transit he's on a flight right now. So kia ora tatou. My name is Julie Shepard. I am the senior environmental planner for Piritako Tribal Authority and I am also the writer of the submissions. So if we can kick into that Piritako Tribal Authority opposes the Māori purposes bill as currently proposed while we acknowledge

the intent to modernize and streamline processes for Māori landowners. We believe the bill lacks sufficient safeguards to protect Māori landowner rights aligned with the principles of Te Tiriti o Waitangi and ensure the long-term retention of whenua Māori. The Māori Affairs Committee holds a critical responsibility to safeguard the interests of whenua Māori. While you have our written submission I must stress the significant risks posed by the proposed amendments to the Māori Trust Act 1953 which forms part of the Māori purposes bill. Our key concerns with the Māori Trust Act

amendment is clause 71 removing the judicial oversight and land transfers which heightens the risk of alienation and undermines the collective decision-making. Clause 85, the simplified land acquisition processes fail to address affordability and cultural appropriateness.

Disadvantaged Māori who already face significant resource limitations. Clause 88 aligning with general land transfer laws erodes Te Kanga Māori and introduces unnecessary legal complexities. 19 replacing the Minister of Māori affairs with a general minister diminishes Māori-specific advocacy.

Course 95, regulatory updates, risk-creating inconsistencies and administrative burdens for under-resourced Māori entities. Together, these changes pose a direct threat to Tēnōranga, Tēnātanga and the long-term retention of whenua Māori. Of course, this bill has broader implications. These risks are compounded by proposed changes to the whenua Māori Act, 1993, developed by Te Puni Kōkiri under the direction of Minister Pōtaka.

These changes further undermine iwi Māori land rights specific concerns include central register of owners' trustees, centralising sensitive information, lease terms, extending lease terms to 99 years without court oversight, locks future generations out of decision-making and access to the whenua. Crown-imposed governance, temporary crown-imposed governance on ungoverned land bypasses collective decision-making in te kāngamārī, enabling external control and eroding Tēnōranga-tērātanga. It becomes

quite overwhelming, I have to say. Whenua Mārī, we all understand, as a tāngatukuiho, integral to Māori identity and well-being, the history of systemic land loss from the New Zealand land wars to the native land court demands vigilance to prevent further alienation. The upcoming referendum framework bill further illustrates these dangers with its silence on Te Tiriti o Waitangi the potential for misinformation campaigns, threatening to bury indigenous rights and silence iwi Māori voices. We only need to look back to understand the consequences of

further steps in stolen land. This is further exacerbated by the ongoing construction of prisons which disproportionately target and incarcerate Māori perpetuating colonial harm and systemic oppression. History has shown us that injustices against iwi Māori remain entrenched in a relentless cycle of repetition rendering crown apologies hollow and devoid of meaning words without transformative action no longer hold significance. When these policies and actions unfold they represent a deliberate continuation of colonial strategies perpetuating the

systematic erosion of Māori rights under te te re te o waitangi. This cannot and must not be accepted. The Māori Affairs Committee has a duty to intervene and halt this destructive trajectory. Failure to act will have devastating consequences and history will not absolve those who stood by and enabled this harm. Pirarako Tribal Authority urges the committee to reject the Māori Purposes Bill in its current form. We call for meaningful engagement with iwi Māori to ensure that any legislative changes uphold the principles of te

te re te o waitangi, protect te no rangatiratanga and safeguard the legacy of iwi Māori for future generations. Ngā mihi nui kia koutou katoa. Thank you for listening tomorrow. Thank you and I'll leave this just a just a couple of minutes for a couple of questions if there are any from members any questions from members at this time.

just thank you for the thorough submission. Yeah it was a very thorough submission Julie so thank you very much for the time that you put into it and we appreciate you coming before us today. Kia ora. Thank you for your time. So when it's Craig it comes in and that's online, thank you.

Tena koe Craig. How are you? Well thank you yourselves. Very good thank you Craig we've got five minutes allocated for your verbal submission where we have that before us of course so if you'd leave a few minutes or a couple of minutes at the end there so that we can ask any questions we may have so away you go Craig. Kia ora thank you. Firstly I'd just like to acknowledge that the Marijan gentleman Tom Harper who will ask me many years ago to attend Mario fairs committees and treaty claims and that. I think the reason why Tom asked

me because Tom knew that I don't go backwards. Tom was a former president of the Black Power and stood over me first time he met me and when I never stepped back that's why Tom thought that I should step forward and speak up for Mario and so forth. I think this I acknowledge that making it easier for the administration of Mario's organisations I've no problem with that but I think the intent is 180 years too late. I don't think legislation could put what Mario's purpose are. I think Mario has been telling environmentally what their

purpose is and telling the Crown for 180 years that you've ignored so I do not think in any way that this legislation can be put together as Mario's purpose bill and so forth. We want to hear from Mario what their purpose is. We don't want to hear what the Crown and the Crown's representation that have a contract just to safeguard the Crown's assets and so forth to put it forward.

We want to hear from Mary and we want to hear from the Marry Queen, what the vision and the vision of what going forward we have. So yeah, that's where I stand on this matter. Okay, thank you very much Craig. Any questions from members?

Hey Craig, just want to say that you've always been very consistent with your position. Really a comment and that's around, I don't know if you've had the opportunity to read any of the annual reports of Iwida, most of them are published online. You might be interested in some of the visions that are articulated within there. And of course, it's not to say that all Māori think the same way and have the same vision, it's not the correct actually. But yeah, I just thought

I'd mention it, see if you might be interested in having a look at them. I think my point though, Adrian, is that how it's portrayed to the park here and the white person, it's probably always done in Māori language and so forth. I attended when the Māori party ended Parliament to the lower Marae there and with two hours of Māori, which referred to me like going to a church in Ireland, I had two hours of all that in mass, you know, uncomprehensible. So I think it's got to be put out in English and a very simple sort of what the

future holds and what Māori would like instead of the other way. around I was told like my first thought of this legislation was it's just dog legislation it's just a crown trying to put Māori into how they want to to treat them. Okay thank you. Okay I thank you very much Craig.
Kia ora, thank you. So next we have John Cifasila.

Kia ora John. Kia ora. Tenakui, thank you very much. We have your brief submission before us John. We've got five minutes allocated for your submission for us today. If you just leave a minute or so at the end there for any questions that may come from members. So where you go John? Okay well so this is the first time I've done this and I'm really I think I think a lot of people are really tired of what government's doing about us Māoris as people of the land and I'm one of those people that grew up in the generation where

I was a half tongue and half Māori but they didn't consider me as a Māori to be in a Māori class and I've been through a lot of rough times with the society trying to find myself as a Māori like this society actually took me away from my culture and I had to take time out to find my way back all by myself you know with all of these I'm really proud that there's Māori out there putting out like stuff for us to learn from and I appreciate that so much because if it wasn't for those people I would have lost my culture so I mean I'm all for the Māoris

like big time man I'm Māori like from blood from birth and all these legislations that China's us. It's unacceptable. It's unfair for our people. I mean, if you guys are really looking to, I mean, if the government's really looking to raise our people, why are they still slamming us?

Why are they still trying to take stuff away from us? And yeah, man, that's the first time. So I don't know if you guys got any questions I'll answer as best as I can. Okay. Well, John, first of all, thank you very much for partaking in the process that we that's important that we do have engagement from people like yourself and many others that makes the system stronger and it's important that you have a voice here.

So I appreciate that. So any questions from members here? Not really a question. Thank you, John. We don't get enough people submitting. So congratulations to you for actually making a submission. I don't want to put you in an awkward position. So happy to talk offline because it sounds like you've been through some trauma and I don't want to force you into actually talking about that. But yeah, happy to contact you if you want to. Yeah, man, cool. Appreciate it.

I know you guys are doing great work, man. Thank you, John. Any last comments from anybody? It appears not. Hey, John, good luck with your journey, mate. We're well done for coming before us today. Kia ora.
Okay, now we're up to Maxine Williams.

So Maxine, we'll just bring you online one second. Can you turn your video on, please, if you're able to? Oh gosh. Here on. Oh, here on. Can... Oh yeah. Yeah. Thank you very much for coming before us today. We have your submission, written submission before us.

We've got five minutes allocated for your time. If you could just leave a middle or so towards the end there for any potential questions from members here. But the floor is yours. Way you go. Thank you. I'm just going to start my thing so I don't go over time.

Right. My name is Maxine Williams. I'm Māori. And I'll just go straight into it. I haven't done this before. So my main thing about this is the change of the word Māori. So 1867, the term Māori in this particular act means a male Aboriginal native inhabitant of New Zealand of the age of 21 years in upwards and shall include half-castes.

Then 1902, the word Māori means an Aboriginal inhabitant of New Zealand and includes half-castes and their descendants by natives. The same thing meant the same thing in 1905 and the electoral 1905 act. Again, the same meaning was applied in 1927 act.

Then we get to the 1956 electoral act and Māori means a person belonging to the Aboriginal race of New Zealand and includes a half-caste and a person intermediate in blood between half-castes and persons of pure descent from that race. And then we come to 1991.

Māori means a person of the Māori race of New Zealand. and includes a descendant of any such person and that was the Ministry of Māori Development Act. Māori means a person of the Māori race of New Zealand and includes any descendant of such a person, 1993 electoral act. My whole point about putting in the submission is that that word hasn't been changed since 1927. It went to 1956 and there was a change and then a change

again in 1991, 1993 and we're changing it again. Granted that you're you're only adding the Macron across the top of the letter A but it appears given what's happening with how people are identifying now. Now my father was born in 1922. Ngāpuhi, Whakapapa's down one line from Hungi Hika and if we go up to Hungi Hika and we go across to Hungi Hika's brother he comes down the other line as well. So 1922 he served in the Māori Battalion. He would be 103 today.

We grew up, I didn't realize what I grew up with and I didn't value that until the last 10 years when I'm watching what I knew as a Māori to be is more is morphing into encompassing you know others and stuff and changing and I really it really bothers me that this is the case it's I mean you've got overseas you've got overseas people wanting to to claim being Māori. Why? And with this little change of the macron above the A, it may seem so simple,

but where does that place me? And then all my 2.0 before me now, are we brought across to be encompassed in a whole other type of what Māori means? I don't know. It just really bothers me. This whole Māori Purpose Bill, I mean, I've had no tertiary education. I left school at the end of third form. Anything I do know is because I've read and I believe that my generation had one of the best years of education ever in this country. I grew up

in South Auckland. At that time, everyone around me, all the Māoris in our area in Manurua, we all had mortgages on our homes. My parents had a mortgage. All of us at school had mortgages. We all had contact back to our marae because my father was the oldest of the oldest. We were always back to Whangarua for tummy. And now, you know, what I'm hearing and what of the lack of or how we were displaced, it's just, it's not true. And it wasn't true for me. And maybe my father being the oldest may have, have given us a bit of privilege. But I don't think so

because all the Māori kids I went to school with, we all went back to our marae. We all knew where we came from. And suddenly it doesn't appear that suddenly I'm hearing that people were misplaced. And I was a correction officer at one stage. Many Māori in prison don't know their whakapapa.

happened to know because of my father's surnames and areas and I had a lot of conversations with our Māori prisoners and I could tell them it's like you start here go looking there I could take some of their whakapapa and some of their names and help them find that and I can still do that today so this whole change as small as it may be is I just feel it's a bit of a threat to where I stand as Māori today so I'm sorry they have to interrupt you we've got a busy schedule today and we've just took past five minutes with regards to your

submission we hear your message and I think I heard that this is your first time in submitting so well done for that and it's great to see new people being involved in the political process of what select committees and this committee here we are truly trying to find ways to have better outcomes for Māori around the place that's our endeavor itself and the submissions like your just that reminds us of the many challenges we still have before us itself unfortunately we don't have any time for questions but Maxine thank you very

much for your submission and also coming for us today Kia ora thank you right Colin hi Colin how are you good we've got sure written submission before us obviously we've got a chance to read that and once again we've got five minutes allocated for you verbally to be able to present and but if you just leave a moment of time at the end there's still our some space for questions if there are any so where you go Colin welcome

Hi I know I can I can't my head talk about a man cut off a matter to talk about that I thought that was a good idea I won't add a great deal to what I've written down because basically that is what I have to say and I've tried to make it as clear as I can.

They are some, in some cases, some highly technical points. You may think that these are the thoughts of an old man with too much time on his hands. And that is true, but I still think that some of the points are worth having a look at. So having said that, I'd be very happy to take any questions that you have.

Thank you. I shall open it up for questions to anybody. I know your history here with Dragas, the Morioris and that type of thing. I've got to be a bit careful because I'm actually not even using it. A bit of history there back in the day, but yeah, it's an interesting point. It's great. Yeah, no, Tena koe.

And I'd always do taki iakoe. And na mahi mo te artifakaro epana ketene kupu imi. Could you just speak a little bit more about that? They're absolutely fascinating, but it's not a word that I had ever encountered before. It was a recommendation of the review done in 2022 into the 2016 Māori Language Act that the status of the Moriori language be recognized by the use of that word in the listing of Kahui iwi in the act.

Now, so much of what is proposed in relation to Te Reo Māori comes from that report. It seemed to me that it was unusual that the bill should have come forward without that change being made and without any explanation. So on the basis of my respect for the people who did the review in 2000.

in 2022, I thought I would submit and say, well, it should go back in. The crown has for a long time now recognized the particular situation of te re moriori and the fact that it is to all intents and purposes as a spoken language largely extinct. But I'm a tremendous admirer of the efforts being made by te re moriori to bring the language back again. And the crown, both parties, leading parties,

have recognized that and supported it. And it seemed to me that this was a minor but significant step. And I just took the authority of the review committee on whose advice most of the changes have been put forward to say, well, it's good enough for them. It certainly good enough for me.

So also because of the care that you've taken in terms of your thoughts, one of the things that this committee is looking at is the wider ecosystem for Te Whakapiawai or Te Reo Maori. And you look at both of them here. So, Tomata Wai and Aroha Mai, is the one that you look at. Could you speak a bit more to your

understanding of the role of Tomata Wai and what role you'd like to see it playing in that ecosystem? Sir, this is with reference to your careful observations in point three. I think that the Temapa Wai is the leader of revitalisation among iwi and Māori, as it says in the act, and of course most particularly the leader of revitalisation among hapū does an awful lot at that level and in urban communities as well, and I think that

Temapa Wai should be respected for the work that it has done, and I think that it's also been responsible for inspiring at the macro level, at the national level, inspiring a focus on language planning, which has been so important as the whole basis of revitalisation. Having said that, the point of my submission here, in terms of the spirit of service to the public, the proposal, which is not in line with the suggestions of the 2022 review, the proposal to remove the spirit of service to the public as one of Temapa Wai's

aims, seems to me to undermine Temapa Wai's contribution to the whole nation. Revitalisation of the Māori language is not of benefit only to iwi and to Māori, they are the owners of the Māori language, there's no doubt about that, nonetheless the existence of the Māori language is, as the act says, of value to the nation, and I felt that the removal of the idea of spirit of service to the public, making Temapa Wai the only organisation that that had ever been actively removed from, put a preposition in the wrong place,

to do that sent an unusual message suggesting that somehow revitalisation was not also for the public because to my mind it is. Unfortunately we're out of time but Colin before you go thank you very much for your very thoughtful submission and very well researched submission and it's something for us to think about what you've contributed today so thank you very much Colin.

But, Mary, welcome, and the floor is yours. Way you go. Greetings, members of the committee. Thank you for having me here today to present this opportunity to speak for you. No ye men by all precinct, I stand before you as a woman, living fresh flesh and breath.

Not a corporate fiction, not a statutory abstraction, but as the private agent and authorised representative of the trust and estate known in commerce as Mary and many of them limited. I speak today under divine law, natural law of a law of nation and universal law with full consciousness of the political and spiritual standing as a national.

the landian and ascendant of the AB originals, the native land-grantors. The co-heirs and living moody whose names and inheritance are not inscribed, not on your roles but in the report of heaven and earth. I do hereby give public notice. All assumed powers of attorney are revoked. Do not consent to contract register perform under your legislative bill without full disclosure, mutual agreement and honorable terms. This moody purpose of omnibus bill poses alterations to language, land and office that trespass upon sacred agreement. You seek to redefine moody through your own construct

placing a macron where no consent was given, thereby displacing the original role of the native trustee and violating the naming rights of lineage. The legal substitution of gendered turn, his, her, or the neutered, there, is a deliberate attack on natural succession and lawful inheritance. This reconfiguration is not linguistic convenience that is legislative warfare.

You attempt to reclassify new monster as te waipounamun without the consent of the original grantors of their living agents. By what lawful authority do you pursue to rename what you did not create? Let it be known, I stand as heir and co-heir and successor to the principal, Samuel Brown, Charlie Brown and Robert Brown who retain all rights, interest and equitable title to native land grants predating 1852. I do not submit those interests to the oversight of your statutory moody trustee nor do I accept that the trust is dissolved,

This bill cloaked in reform is in fact a seizure, a subtle removal of ancient signposts and lines of authority. What is your comprehension regarding the use of reo Māori, not te reo Māori? What authorises you to alter the spiritual tongue of a people and to mummetise and understatute?

The reo Māori belongs not to the crown, not to departments but to the breath and record of ancestral lines. To this committee, I say plainly, we may have jurisdiction over legal persons but not over the living. I appear in peace and in good faith and with clean hands.

Let this record stand, monk protunk, reserved in perpetuity. I am of the family brown, private agent of the principal, co-aware, lawful successor and authorised voice of living, estate and trust, your most obedient servant. Alongside this, and in conjunction with this, I remain in honour without vexation, without waiver, without acquiescence, that justice, not legislation, be done.

Kia ora. Kia ora, Mary. That's just enough time for Caps 1 question. Any questions from any of the members? Thank you. We're all right, OK. Okay, I'm just gonna read directly and I'll read fast to get through.

I'd just like to say, just as New Zealand Māori have the right to attend the Westminster Parliament, to place grievances, we descendants of the Ngāti Pākehā peoples that have been in this country for many hundreds of years have the right to ask, what are the Māori obligations to the AB Original Pākehā inhabitants?

According to the Severe Weather Emergency Act, any order must not grant an exemption from or modify a requirement or restriction imposed by the Bill of Rights 1688. New Zealand, particularly New Munster, South Island, is a Protestant Christian nation by the First Laws of the Land pursuant to Jesus Christ of Nazareth and New Zealand continues to be bound to international agreements made by John Ward Esquire Commissioner of her Britannic Majesty in 1841 to certain German immigrants to retain full enjoyment of their national rights,

usages, customs and Lutheran Protestant laws. All Māori entities, corporate or otherwise, have an obligation to continue the legacy of King Te Reo Anga Anga, King Werowero, Tamati Waka Nene, Godfide Sixtus, Queen Victoria, King William IV and Weremo Ratana to maintain international agreements made to...

Germany to protect their Protestant right to the white slaves brought here by the order of the native chiefs. In this case, the Committee in Part 3, testing of legislative content for consistency with New Zealand's international obligations have failed because they did not observe any or even think that international obligations applied.

New Munster, the South Island, as the AB, original German inhabitants named it, cannot change its name to Te Waipaunamo without referendum. The electoral laws of the area defined in command that the district of New Munster and Icaroa be deemed one district alone.

New Munster has its own seal and New Ulster its own seal. Any attempts to place New Munster under the northern district of Takitimu would be a violation of international agreements between Germany and New Zealand and a forfeiture of my person without consent.

The people in the area of Polydorf, Ranzau, Motere and Machuaika retain their native German language customs and proprietary rights and hereditments by derivative titles as promised by the native kings and I oppose any bills to the contrary on the behalf of those peoples to remain an independent territory from the Northern Territory objection to false attestation I declare that all past decisions of meetings that did not comply with the law of the Maori Community Development Act be deemed invalid and unlawful and fines to be paid

according to section 42 penalties of the Act 1962. The attempt by this committee to use Parliamentary privilege to declare past members of trust right to vote or conduct meetings contrary to the regulations may be a breach of Section 1 of the Bill of Rights 1688, as was clear in Fixed General 1st Muldoon, that the pretended power of suspending of laws or the execution of laws by regal authority without consent of Parliament is illegal.

Objection. I do pray that the removal of the words in Wellington and nature of the business requiring board members to be present at such location of the earth at the time may affect the requirements of the tender of the Crown assembled at Westminster. New Munster is directly bound to Port Nicholson by deed and Tectority O Waitangi the New Munster Treaty and incorporated at Westminster.

So such action is a forfeiture of my person right to know the area of land that the board or chairman are performing the nature of the business and what the nature of the business is. The last section finally the Marryland in the South is currently part of a dispute and claim of interest by myself Parkha Aboriginal Inhabitant Catherine Sixtus and Mary Amelia Brown at the Waitangi Tribunal concerning international agreements between the Government of the German Reich and the Government of New Zealand regarding the release of property rights and interests of German nationals. Namely Article 1

and Article 2 that the New Zealand Government and German Reich agreed to release and transfer property and chiddles to the original German owners and persons deriving titles through them. All Marry entities must give affect to their obligations to such international covenants, which include tangible and intangible hair red detamines signed by native chiefs during the years of 1838 Provisional Bell, 1841 John Walt Ward, 1920 and 1929 of the German Reich. Such properties currently in the process of claim at the Waitangi Tribunal is respective of all land and chattels

pertaining to New Munster, Paulie Dorf, Ranzel and the Wairau Rua Pukie, Akaroa and Chinam island. A repeal of section 101d powers of the board with respect to land and chattels may be a forfeiture of my person and an interference in ancient international treatises annexed to my person. The committee can just bring your can you bring your submission to a close place because we're just going. The committee has failed to consider Treaty of Waitangi North and New Munster Treaty called Tettoriti and has failed to consider international obligations.

Therefore, until the committee corrects its errors, the bill ought not pass. I am Catherine Sixters of the Family Sixters and revoke all your powers to pass this bill until such travesties of justice have been corrected. That's it. your verbal submission. Kia ora Catherine. Right right so we're still here you can go talk to him but it sounds like you might miss it. Okay that's right. Okay so uh Rauawa, a month's worth of charitable trust. Oh, Tena Korua. Kia ora. We can hear you loud and clear.

Thank you very much for coming before us. We've actually got 10 minutes allocated for you to present today. We've got your written submission before us, so take that as read. And you can use this time as you wish to. But just leave a moment of time towards the end of that 10 minutes for any questions that may come from the membership.

Tena Korua. Kia ora i te Komiti. Tena matau o Rauawa Kaumatua Charitable Trust. Ko Wati Greening a hau. E tu nei hau e raro i te maru o Ngā Kaumatua e te rohi nei o mātau hau ta kumanaua. Ta kumanaua. This bill seeks to modernise grievance structures for Māori and we totoka that vision, but we also caution if it's not built within kōmātua in mind, it risks leaving them behind.

He whakatu pato na maatau. Don't modernise by marginalising. Kanui te mi. Kia ora kaita katoa. Kanui te mihi kia kaitai. Kōrangi mawhura hau. Kōai te tūmua kia ora. Awa awa and it's our privilege to be able to present our oral submission this morning.

So tēnā koutou. I'll take our letter as read in that and in essence, some of the key things for us is digital exclusion of our Kaumatua. So while we support the attempt to modernise for our Māori purposes bill, what we do want to ensure is that those...

lived experience and wisdom are not marginalised in the process, digital exclusion is something that we know as a provider on the ground is a reality for many of our kaumatua and what we also know is that they hold historical knowledge that will often be quite influential in terms of the well-being in the future of the kaupapa at hand and kaumatua want to participate so ensuring that we enable that whilst also looking after the future of the kaupapa being dealt with under the Māori Purposes Bill is quite critical.

We have two other colleagues that we're meant to join, I wonder can you see them and we can't. A Pare Miha and a Shabana ready? Can we bring them on as well? We're just bringing them online now. Oh thank you so much.
